author: @skampp
text: It's funny that Stratasys comes up.  I rough-printed a typical radial flux generator that was < 200mm x 200mm x 150mm, all in all it took about 1.5 rolls of filament so about $35 USD.  It did take quite a bit of time to print all in all.  I was curious what it would cost to have a company do the printing for me, just to save the time, so I got a quote from those guys.  After uploading my parts and getting a quick consult, I was quoted $2,386 USD.  So I suppose there's money to be made if you're good at this.

author: @bukszpryt_
text:
I have a Neptune 2s and it works fine. After first bed leveling it just works without any bigger problems.

Unfortunatelly it's a bit of lottery with all these chinese printers. There are units that are pretty much plug and play and there are others that require more work and modifications to produce satisfying results.
For me the PEI sheet on Neptune is the best printing surface i ever used, but i was in conntact with people who tried everything and couldn't get the print to stick to it and they just replaced it with glass.

I must say that elegoo has pretty good support. The SD card i received with my printer was corrupted and they sent me new one with no problem at all.

author: @crankfwd8209
text:
I would like your opinions on this idea I have for an underground electricity generating system. There is a reservoir of ferrofluid at the top of the system, from which a tube goes downwards about 500metres where it drives a turbine. The ferrofluid then collects in a bottom reservoir. I then have thousands of tubes (straws), immersed in the ferrofluid, that go upwards for about 34feet where they curve back downwards for a couple of inches. Underneath the downward curved end of every tube, is a magnet, which pulls the ferrofluid onto itself forming a nice prickly blob. The magnet can only hold so much ferrofluid, and the excess will fall into yet another reservoir just beneath the magnet. This reservoir has thousands of tubes leading up to thousands of magnets, filling yet another reservoir about thirty feet above. I repeat this process until I fill a reservoir that is above the original reservoir, which it feeds. 
Obviously the tubes and reservoirs would have to be pre-filled before the system would start working and it would be a nightmare to build, but is it possible? How high can a magnet pull ferrofluid? I've heard that neodymium magnets can last 700 years, so have I just invented a clean energy source?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have no idea mate - if I wanted to answer your questions I would build a model to see - why don't you do that?

replies.author: @crankfwd8209
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Because I found out how much ferrofluid costs😂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @crankfwd8209 you can make you own you know - it's pretty easy and cheap

replies.author: @crankfwd8209
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I'm not too good with stuff like that, the only recipe I know is for disaster.

author: @johnmarkgatti3324
text: Ah ha , so Robert is allowed in the kitchen with sharp objects ..we have wondered ! ?.  I bought DDD stocks ,sadly after the initial excitement , had to wait a long time to break even . now have rather a lot of TTT shares , Aussie company ,Titomech,with govt 'protection' , printing in 'cheap' titanium powder useing a metal deposition proccess . This is fast ,needs no sintering , with shrinkage problems , is proven seriously strong , no layer weakness, [ has been signed off for all aero space uses ]. and scaleable ..ie a ship anyone ?. They have done a 5 meter rocket , are also doing very fancey rifle barrels [ which need even fancier cooling built in , as T is a seriously bad heat conductor ]. The tech also lends it self to mixing metals ,a whole conversation !. My hope is this will eventually allow, serious at home ,or small bussiness , printing , anywhere in the world ,of space age tech parts for anything ,upgrades or replacements . I asked them to make tube in any down time , they now do tube ,for oil/harsh enviroments ,with a 1000 year garantee . Imagine a titanium wind or water energy harvesting turbine ,or seriously fancy chairs .. or kitchen knives ,that need no sharpening !? a gold Titanium alloy is [ so far as i know at this stage ] the hardest metal .  And Crickey ,do you have a UPS on when printing for a week ?. faith indeed . { and thanks for another great update Robert ].

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would be game changing if that went to the consumer market

author: @grendel1960a
text: I started with one of the first ready out of the box printers, the Davinci 1.0 Aio at the end of 2016, it literally arrived in a big box, you took it out of the box, plugged it in, loaded the filament and off. fully enclosed, I have to say it works with ABS perfectly. then to supplement that and for PLA i got the FLsun Q5 which has a circular print bed. both have an 8" working area, the davinci is a cube, the flsun is an 82 cylinder (but in reality its a tapered cylinder as it cant really fully reach the extremities at the top of the z axis.)

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Thanks for the video.  You might be interested in printing 3D metal parts using a sintering process. Please take a look at this video by Integza: How I 3D Printed a Metal Aerospike Rocket at Home. You can 3d print metal parts and sinter them using a metal filled filaments by companies such as Virtual Foundry. These do require a Kiln to sinter after the print is complete. You can make somewhat complex metal parts that would be otherwise difficult for you to machine at your shop.

author: @lagunafishing
text: Hi Rob, have you ever dabbled in designing custom prints? I spent the best part of a year learning CAD and print with an ender3. I finally settled on Autodesk Fusion 360 modelling software. It's fully functional and free to use for 'mature' students :)

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: Rob does have a handy CAD user (ex Drawing Office manager) available if custom parts are needed (me) I use fusion 360 (the hobbyist version- limited to 10 active models) I can draw up pretty much anything he can describe to me. he has expressed an interest in learning CAD to design his own parts.

replies.author: @johnmarkgatti3324
replies.text: really good to know .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I haven't yet mate - but it is on the list for sure

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: All these expensive softwares are not worth learning. The free version get scrapped or downgraded all the time. Consider learning Blender instead. You can design pretty much any 3D model from scratch once you get the hang off it, plenty of YT tutorials. It's good stuff and free.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @DKFX1 having designed stuff in two of the most expensive CAD systems out there all my life, I find the free version of fusion 360 the nearest to what I know, and every time I have tried to use a lot of the stuff like blender, i have found it limiting to what I want to do, thats not to say its no good, but when I know the function I want to use, and its not apparently there, I get frustrated. With nearly 40 years CAD experience I tend to fall back on what I know, and fusion 360 is the closest to that I have found, generally I can convert over to any true CAD system in a matter of days as iits justa matter of finding what the common commands are now called. if you are learning from scratch then probably some of these other systems would be a good place to start.

author: @naturesmoments1297
text: Hello again Rob and the team, having recently received and installed one of the Chinese diesel heaters my first thought was how to capture heat from the exhaust outlet, only mentioning this as its such an easy way to improve overall heat out put at no extra running cost, lots of ideas including heating a bucket of water or running through a standard radiator - on the look out for a second hand hot water cylinder with coil, this would take a while to heat but great heat store 👍

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: I have a video of doing that exactly and successfully using an old cast iron steam radiator.  I cooked in a pan and heated buckets of water using the waste heat-which is substantial.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: I use the concrete block from an old washing machine drum as a heat store in my small log burner to spread the heat output over a longer time period.

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text:
@grendel1960a This needs to be sealed as it's low pressure but high volume exhaust from a 1" exhaust pipe of ~700 degree exhaust gas.  The steam cast iron radiator acts as a good store of heat as they weigh between 100-700 lbs (I've moved them all as I used to empty hoarder homes for 15 years)
I've also used a heat exchanger from a car which gets about 500 degrees, a copper radiator from a car which gets around 400 degrees near the intake port.  It's fun to recover heat from all sources, I put a cast iron pot with water that weighs 70lbs full on my Jotul wood stove in the garage.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that mate - maybe a sand battery?

replies.author: @naturesmoments1297
replies.text: Just wanted to add, those first two ideas were from YouTube videos, these little heaters are becoming so popular, my neighbour just texted me to ask if she can install one to help with draughty cottage 👌
